news_summary: Scientists have made breakthroughs in cancer treatment, vaccine benefits, muscle aging reversal, and vitiligo therapy by targeting underlying biological processes to improve health outcomes.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont réalisé des avancées majeures dans le traitement du cancer, les bienfaits des vaccins, le rajeunissement musculaire et le traitement du vitiligo en ciblant les processus biologiques sous-jacents afin d'améliorer les résultats en matière de santé.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han logrado avances importantes en el tratamiento del cáncer, los beneficios de las vacunas, la reversión del envejecimiento muscular y la terapia del vitíligo, centrándose en los procesos biológicos subyacentes para mejorar los resultados de salud.

articles.title: Scientists engineer bacteria to eat cancer tumors from the inside out
articles.summary: Researchers are engineering bacteria to invade tumors and consume them from the inside. Because tumor cores lack oxygen, they’re the perfect breeding ground for these microbes. The team added a genetic tweak that helps the bacteria survive longer near oxygen-exposed edges — but only once enough of them are present to trigger the change. It’s a carefully programmed biological attack that could one day offer a new way to destroy cancer.
articles.content:
Scientists at the University of Waterloo are working on a new cancer treatment that uses specially engineered bacteria to consume tumors from the inside. The strategy relies on microbes that naturally thrive in oxygen-free environments, which makes the interior of many solid tumors an ideal target.

"Bacteria spores enter the tumor, finding an environment where there are lots of nutrients and no oxygen, which this organism prefers, and so it starts eating those nutrients and growing in size," said Dr. Marc Aucoin, a chemical engineering professor at Waterloo. "So, we are now colonizing that central space, and the bacterium is essentially ridding the body of the tumor."

At the center of this approach is Clostridium sporogenes, a bacterium commonly found in soil. It can survive only in places that contain absolutely no oxygen. The inner core of solid tumors is made up of dead cells and lacks oxygen, creating the perfect conditions for this microbe to multiply and spread.

Overcoming the Oxygen Barrier

There is a challenge, however. As the bacteria expand outward and reach areas of the tumor exposed to small amounts of oxygen, they begin to die off before fully eliminating the cancer.

To address this limitation, the team inserted a gene from a related bacterium that is more tolerant of oxygen. This modification allows the engineered microbes to survive longer near the tumor's outer regions.

The researchers also needed a way to control when that oxygen-tolerance feature turns on. Activating it too early could allow the bacteria to grow in oxygen-rich areas such as the bloodstream, which would be unsafe. To prevent that, they used a natural bacterial communication process called quorum sensing.

Quorum sensing relies on chemical signals released by bacteria. As their numbers increase, the signal grows stronger. Only after enough bacteria have accumulated inside a tumor does the signal reach a level that switches on the oxygen-resistant gene. This timing ensures the bacteria activate their survival mechanism only when it is needed.

Synthetic Biology and DNA Circuits

In an earlier study, the team showed that Clostridium sporogenes could be genetically altered to better withstand oxygen. In a follow-up experiment, they tested their quorum sensing design by programming bacteria to produce a green fluorescent protein, allowing them to confirm that the system activated at the intended moment.

"Using synthetic biology, we built something like an electrical circuit, but instead of wires we used pieces of DNA," said Dr. Brian Ingalls, a professor of applied mathematics at Waterloo. "Each piece has its job. When assembled correctly, they form a system that works in a predictable way."

The next step is to combine both the oxygen-tolerance gene and the quorum-sensing control system into a single bacterium and evaluate it against tumors in pre-clinical trials.

Collaboration Driving Cancer Innovation

This research began with work by PhD student Bahram Zargar under the supervision of Ingalls and Dr. Pu Chen, a retired professor of chemical engineering at Waterloo. The project highlights the university's focus on interdisciplinary health innovation, bringing together experts in engineering, mathematics, and life sciences to translate scientific discoveries into real-world medical solutions.

The Waterloo team is collaborating with the Center for Research on Environmental Microbiology (CREM Co Labs), a Toronto company co-founded by Dr. Zargar. The partnership also includes Dr. Sara Sadr, a former Waterloo doctoral student who played a leading role in advancing the research.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where engineered bacteria are designed to selectively invade and consume cancer tumors from the inside, potentially offering a novel and effective cancer treatment. The research addresses a major health challenge with a sophisticated biological approach, involving genetic engineering and quorum sensing to target tumors precisely. The impact is broad and meaningful for cancer patients worldwide, and the article provides detailed context on the scientific innovation and collaborative efforts.
articles.personality_title: Scientists engineer bacteria to eat cancer tumors from the inside out
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Cancer tumors often have areas in their center without oxygen, making them hard to treat with traditional methods. Researchers have been looking for new ways to target these tough-to-reach parts.

**What happened** – Scientists at the University of Waterloo have modified a type of bacteria called Clostridium sporogenes to live inside tumors and eat them from within. These bacteria naturally grow only where there is no oxygen, like the inside of tumors. The team added a gene that helps the bacteria survive near the edges of tumors where some oxygen is present. They also programmed the bacteria to only activate this gene when enough of them gather inside the tumor, using a method called quorum sensing, which works like a bacterial communication system.

**Impact** – This is important because it allows the bacteria to attack the tumor safely and effectively without harming healthy parts of the body. The bacteria’s ability to survive in oxygen and control their behavior means they can destroy more of the tumor than before. This approach offers a new type of treatment that could work where other therapies fail.

**What's next step** – The researchers will now combine the oxygen-survival gene and the quorum sensing system into one bacterium and test it in pre-clinical trials. This will help them see how well the bacteria work in living organisms and prepare for possible future use in cancer patients.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have created smart bacteria that can safely eat cancer tumors from the inside, offering a promising new way to treat cancer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques modifient des bactéries pour dévorer les tumeurs cancéreuses de l’intérieur
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les tumeurs cancéreuses ont souvent un centre sans oxygène, ce qui les rend difficiles à traiter avec les méthodes classiques. Les chercheurs cherchent de nouvelles façons de cibler ces zones difficiles.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université de Waterloo ont modifié une bactérie appelée Clostridium sporogenes pour qu’elle vive à l’intérieur des tumeurs et les dévore. Ces bactéries poussent naturellement là où il n’y a pas d’oxygène, comme au centre des tumeurs. L’équipe a ajouté un gène qui aide la bactérie à survivre près des bords de la tumeur où il y a un peu d’oxygène. Ils ont aussi programmé les bactéries pour qu’elles activent ce gène seulement quand elles sont assez nombreuses dans la tumeur, grâce à un système de communication bactérienne appelé quorum sensing.

**Impact** – Cela permet aux bactéries d’attaquer la tumeur en toute sécurité et de façon efficace, sans abîmer les parties saines du corps. Leur capacité à survivre en présence d’oxygène et à contrôler leur comportement leur permet de détruire plus de la tumeur. Cette méthode pourrait devenir un nouveau traitement quand les autres échouent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont maintenant combiner le gène de survie à l’oxygène et le système de quorum sensing dans une seule bactérie et la tester lors d’essais précliniques. Cela leur permettra de vérifier l’efficacité dans des organismes vivants et de préparer une utilisation future chez les patients.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont créé des bactéries intelligentes capables de dévorer les tumeurs cancéreuses de l’intérieur, offrant une nouvelle piste prometteuse pour le traitement du cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos diseñan bacterias que devoran tumores cancerosos desde adentro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los tumores cancerosos suelen tener un centro sin oxígeno, lo que dificulta su tratamiento con métodos tradicionales. Los investigadores buscan nuevas formas de atacar estas áreas difíciles.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Waterloo modificaron una bacteria llamada Clostridium sporogenes para que viva dentro de los tumores y los consuma. Estas bacterias crecen naturalmente donde no hay oxígeno, como en el interior de los tumores. El equipo añadió un gen que ayuda a la bacteria a sobrevivir cerca de los bordes del tumor donde hay algo de oxígeno. También programaron a las bacterias para que solo activen este gen cuando hay suficientes dentro del tumor, usando un sistema de comunicación bacteriana llamado quorum sensing.

**Impacto** – Esto permite que las bacterias ataquen el tumor de forma segura y efectiva, sin dañar partes sanas del cuerpo. Su capacidad para sobrevivir con oxígeno y controlar su comportamiento les ayuda a destruir más del tumor. Este método ofrece un nuevo tipo de tratamiento que podría funcionar donde otros fallan.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores combinarán el gen de tolerancia al oxígeno y el sistema de quorum sensing en una sola bacteria y la probarán en ensayos preclínicos. Esto les ayudará a ver cómo funciona en organismos vivos y prepararse para un posible uso en pacientes.

**Frase clave** – Científicos han creado bacterias inteligentes que pueden devorar tumores cancerosos desde adentro, ofreciendo una forma prometedora de tratar el cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of glowing, friendly-looking bacteria shaped like tiny, softly illuminated spores gently surrounding and breaking down a stylized, semi-abstract tumor represented as a dark, textured core with nutrient-rich layers inside, set against a simple, natural color background that evokes a nurturing internal environment, with subtle glowing signals between the bacteria symbolizing their communication and coordinated action.

articles.title: The surprising vaccine side effects that can improve long-term health
articles.summary: People often focus on the bad side effects of vaccines, but they can have some great side effects too, says columnist Michael Le Page. They don’t just protect us from contagious diseases but can also lower the risk of dementia and heart attacks
articles.content:
People often focus on the bad side effects of vaccines, but they can have some great side effects too, says columnist Michael Le Page. They don’t just protect us from contagious diseases but can also lower the risk of dementia and heart attacks

A woman receiving the measles vaccine in Mexico JULIO CESAR AGUILAR/AFP via Getty Images

I am currently arranging to get a shingles vaccination. I had shingles at university and still bear the scars, so I really don’t want to risk getting it again. But I also have in mind the fact that the benefits of the shingles vaccine are not limited to preventing shingles. And this isn’t unusual. We’re discovering that many vaccines have benefits that go way beyond protecting against a single virus or bacterium – facts that are not nearly as widely known as they should be.

Let’s start with shingles. A study of more than a million people last year reported that those given the Zostavax shingles vaccine were 26 per cent less likely to die from heart disease or experience a stroke, heart attack or heart failure during an average of six years after the shot – a massive reduction in risk for such a simple, cheap and easy intervention.

What’s more, people given a newer shingles vaccine called Shingrix were 17 less likely to develop dementia in the next six years compared with those given Zostavax. Since several studies have shown that Zostavax also reduces the risk of dementia, the overall reduction in risk with Shingrix should be even larger. Several other vaccines, including those that protect against flu and tuberculosis, also appear to reduce the risk of dementia.

In the US and Australia, shingles vaccination is recommended for those over 50 or adults with weakened immune systems. Unless you fall into that last category in the UK, it’s only available through public healthcare to those aged 70 to 79. I don’t want to wait that long, so will have to go private.

Shingles is caused by the varicella zoster virus, which causes chickenpox when it first infects us in childhood and then lies dormant in nerve cells, causing shingles when reactivated. So it’s possible vaccinating children against chickenpox will have some benefits later in life, too, though I couldn’t find any studies looking at this.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Flu vaccines also have cardiovascular benefits. For instance, an analysis combining data from more than 9000 people involved in six trials found that those vaccinated against flu had a 34 per cent lower risk of a heart attack or stroke in the next year. The benefit was even greater for people with a recent history of heart problems.

The new vaccines for RSV, or respiratory syncytial virus, seem to help prevent heart problems, too. A study involving around 130,000 people over 60 found that those who had one kind of RSV vaccine were less likely to be hospitalised with heart or lung issues than those who went unvaccinated.

Then there’s the covid-19 mRNA vaccines. They turn out to boost the immune response to tumours in conjunction with immunotherapy, extending peoples’ lives.

I could keep listing examples, but let’s turn instead to why so many vaccines have these wider benefits. We don’t know the precise mechanisms for sure, but it’s not that surprising that they do.

Viruses can do lasting physical damage to the body, and the immune response to them can also harm tissues. So-called cytokine storms, in which the body overreacts and releases too many immune-stimulating signals, are often what kill people with infections such as flu or covid-19, for instance, and there’s growing evidence that multiple sclerosis is a result of the immune response to the Epstein-Barr virus.

Many viruses also target our immune system in various ways to help evade it, weakening our defences. HIV takes this strategy to the extreme, killing it off altogether.

Illustration of human papilloma virus (HPV), which can cause cervical cancer KATERYNA KON/SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY/Alamy

There are also viruses that we can’t get rid of once they infect us, such as the varicella zoster virus. Some, such as human papillomavirus (HPV), even genetically modify our cells by inserting their genomes into ours – this is why HPV causes cancers.

The point is, even viral infections that we tend to regard as harmless, such as flu in young people, can have long-lasting effects that are not always obviously linked to those infections. Studies of long covid have helped raise awareness of this – and of the benefits of vaccines in reducing the risk of lingering harm.

There’s this idea in some circles that “natural immunity” from getting a disease is somehow better than vaccination. It’s bonkers for all the reasons above and more, and measles is a great example of why.

Measles vaccines have cut the number of deaths from the disease from more than 2 million a year before 1980 to under 100,000 in 2024. They have also had the unexpected effect of reducing the number of children dying from other infectious diseases.

Why? Part of the answer is that measles kills off some immune cells, leaving children more vulnerable to a wide range of infections for years afterwards. But measles vaccines also seem to train the immune system in a way that boosts its wider effectiveness. This training effect is so beneficial it’s even been suggested that we should keep giving children the measles vaccine even if measles is eliminated.

Of course, it would be too good to be true if all vaccines trained our immune systems in this way. There is evidence that a few vaccines have the opposite effect. But this doesn’t mean they don’t still save lives overall.

As ever with vaccines, the key question to ask is whether you are better off getting vaccinated than not getting vaccinated. To make an informed choice, you need to balance the risks of infection versus vaccination, taking into account the wider benefits of a vaccine too. All too often the focus is entirely on very rare or entirely imaginary adverse effects of vaccines.

I have no doubt what will make me better off. I’ll be getting my shingles vaccine, and a flu jab every year, and the RSV vaccine when it’s offered, at the very least.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights scientifically supported, broad health benefits of vaccines beyond their primary purpose, such as reducing risks of dementia, heart attacks, and other serious conditions. It provides detailed evidence from large studies and discusses the wider positive impact on public health, encouraging informed vaccination decisions.
articles.personality_title: Vaccines offer surprising long-term health benefits beyond disease protection
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Vaccines are usually known for protecting people from infections like measles or flu. However, recent research shows they can also improve overall health in unexpected ways.

**What happened** – Studies have found that vaccines for shingles, flu, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), and even COVID-19 not only prevent those infections but also lower risks of heart attacks, strokes, and dementia. For example, the shingles vaccine Zostavax reduced heart-related deaths and illnesses by 26%, while the newer shingles vaccine Shingrix lowered dementia risk by 17%. Flu vaccines reduced heart attack or stroke risk by about 34% in the year after vaccination.

**Impact** – These findings reveal that vaccines can strengthen the immune system beyond fighting specific diseases. Some viruses cause lasting damage or weaken the immune system, so vaccination helps prevent this harm. The measles vaccine also protects children from other infections by keeping their immune cells healthy. This shows vaccines have wider benefits that many people do not know about.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue studying how vaccines help protect overall health and which ones provide the best extra benefits. Meanwhile, health experts encourage people, especially older adults, to get recommended vaccines like shingles and flu shots to gain these wider protections. More awareness about these benefits may help more people choose vaccination.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Vaccines do more than stop infections—they also reduce risks of serious conditions like heart disease and dementia, offering important long-term health benefits.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les vaccins offrent des bénéfices surprenants pour la santé à long terme au-delà de la protection contre les maladies
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les vaccins sont généralement connus pour protéger contre des infections comme la rougeole ou la grippe. Cependant, des recherches récentes montrent qu'ils peuvent aussi améliorer la santé globale de façon inattendue.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des études ont montré que les vaccins contre le zona, la grippe, le virus respiratoire syncytial (VRS) et même la COVID-19 préviennent non seulement ces infections mais réduisent aussi les risques d’attaques cardiaques, d’AVC et de démence. Par exemple, le vaccin contre le zona Zostavax a réduit de 26 % les décès et maladies cardiaques, tandis que le vaccin plus récent Shingrix a diminué de 17 % le risque de démence. Les vaccins contre la grippe ont réduit d’environ 34 % le risque d’attaque cardiaque ou d’AVC dans l’année suivant la vaccination.

**Impact** – Ces résultats montrent que les vaccins renforcent le système immunitaire au-delà de la lutte contre une maladie spécifique. Certains virus causent des dommages durables ou affaiblissent le système immunitaire, donc la vaccination aide à prévenir ces effets. Le vaccin contre la rougeole protège aussi les enfants d’autres infections en maintenant leurs cellules immunitaires en bonne santé. Cela montre des bénéfices plus larges que beaucoup ignorent.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier comment les vaccins protègent la santé globale et lesquels offrent les meilleurs bénéfices supplémentaires. En attendant, les experts recommandent aux personnes, surtout âgées, de se faire vacciner contre le zona et la grippe pour profiter de ces protections. Une meilleure information pourrait encourager plus de personnes à choisir la vaccination.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les vaccins font plus que prévenir les infections : ils réduisent aussi les risques de maladies graves comme les problèmes cardiaques et la démence, offrant des bénéfices importants à long terme.
articles.personality_title_es: Las vacunas ofrecen sorprendentes beneficios para la salud a largo plazo más allá de la protección contra enfermedades
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las vacunas suelen ser conocidas por proteger contra infecciones como el sarampión o la gripe. Sin embargo, investigaciones recientes muestran que también pueden mejorar la salud general de formas inesperadas.

**Qué pasó** – Estudios han encontrado que las vacunas contra el herpes zóster, la gripe, el virus respiratorio sincitial (VRS) e incluso el COVID-19 no solo previenen esas infecciones, sino que también reducen riesgos de ataques cardíacos, accidentes cerebrovasculares y demencia. Por ejemplo, la vacuna contra el herpes zóster Zostavax redujo en un 26 % las muertes y enfermedades cardíacas, mientras que la vacuna más nueva Shingrix disminuyó en un 17 % el riesgo de demencia. Las vacunas contra la gripe redujeron el riesgo de ataque cardíaco o accidente cerebrovascular en un 34 % durante el año siguiente.

**Impacto** – Estos hallazgos muestran que las vacunas fortalecen el sistema inmunológico más allá de combatir una enfermedad específica. Algunos virus causan daños duraderos o debilitan el sistema inmunológico, por lo que la vacunación ayuda a prevenir estos daños. La vacuna contra el sarampión también protege a los niños de otras infecciones al mantener saludables sus células inmunitarias. Esto muestra beneficios más amplios que muchas personas desconocen.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando cómo las vacunas protegen la salud general y cuáles ofrecen los mejores beneficios adicionales. Mientras tanto, los expertos en salud animan a las personas, especialmente a los adultos mayores, a recibir las vacunas recomendadas como la del herpes zóster y la gripe para obtener estas protecciones. Mayor conocimiento sobre estos beneficios podría ayudar a que más personas elijan vacunarse.

**Frase clave** – Las vacunas hacen más que evitar infecciones: también reducen riesgos de enfermedades graves como problemas cardíacos y demencia, ofreciendo importantes beneficios a largo plazo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a protective shield formed from intertwined vaccine vials and gentle, glowing nerve-like branches, surrounding symbolic heart and brain shapes that radiate health and vitality, set against a soft, natural color palette.

articles.title: Scientists reverse muscle aging in mice and discover a surprising catch
articles.summary: A UCLA study in mice reveals that aging muscle stem cells accumulate a protein that slows repair but boosts survival. This protein, NDRG1, acts like a brake, preventing cells from activating quickly after injury. When researchers blocked it in older mice, muscle healing sped up dramatically — but stem cells became less resilient over time. The work suggests aging may reflect a survival trade-off rather than straightforward decline.
articles.content:
Aging muscles heal more slowly after injury, a frustrating reality familiar to many older adults.

New research from UCLA, conducted in mice, points to a surprising explanation. As muscles age, their stem cells build up high levels of a protein that makes them slower to switch on and repair damaged tissue. At the same time, that same protein helps the cells survive longer in the more stressful environment of older muscle.

The study, published in the journal Science, suggests that some biological changes linked to aging may not simply be harmful decline. Instead, they may represent built in survival strategies. "This has led us to a new way of thinking about aging," said Dr. Thomas Rando, senior author of the study and director of the Eli and Edythe Broad Center of Regenerative Medicine and Stem Cell Research at UCLA.

"It's counterintuitive, but the stem cells that make it through aging may actually be the least functional ones. They survive not because they're the best at their job, but because they're the best at surviving. That gives us a completely different lens for understanding why tissues decline with age."

The Protein That Slows Muscle Repair With Age

The research team, led by postdoctoral scholars Jengmin Kang and Daniel Benjamin, compared muscle stem cells from young and old mice. They found that a protein called NDRG1 increased sharply with age, reaching levels 3.5 times higher in older cells than in younger ones. NDRG1 acts like a brake inside the cell. It dampens a signaling pathway known as mTOR, which normally drives cells to activate, grow, and repair tissue.

To determine whether NDRG1 was responsible for slower healing, the scientists allowed mice to age naturally to the equivalent of about 75 human years. They then blocked the activity of NDRG1. Once this protein was inhibited, the older muscle stem cells began behaving more like young ones. They activated more quickly and repaired injured muscle faster.

Rejuvenation Comes With a Trade Off

There was a downside. When NDRG1 was blocked, fewer muscle stem cells survived over time. As a result, the muscle's ability to regenerate after repeated injuries was reduced.

"Think of it like a marathon runner versus a sprinter," said Rando, who is also a professor of neurology at the David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA. "The stem cells in young animals are hyper-functioning -- really good at what they do, namely sprinting, but they're not good for the long term. They can make it through the 100-yard dash, but they can't make it even halfway through the marathon. By contrast, aged stem cells are like marathon runners -- slower to respond, but better equipped for the long haul. However, what makes them so proficient over long distances is exactly what renders them poor at sprinting."

The team confirmed their results using several different methods. They studied muscle stem cells from both young and old mice in lab dishes and inside living tissue. Across experiments, the pattern was consistent. Higher levels of NDRG1 slowed stem cell activation and muscle repair, but also strengthened the cells' long term survival.

A Cellular Survival Bias in Aging

The researchers propose that rising NDRG1 levels reflect what they call a "cellular survivorship bias." Over time, stem cells that fail to produce enough NDRG1 are more likely to die. The remaining population is made up of cells that are slower to act but better able to withstand the stresses of aging.

"Some age-related changes that look detrimental -- like slower tissue repair -- may actually be necessary compromises that prevent something worse: the complete depletion of the stem cell pool," Rando said.

Rando compares this shift to survival trade offs seen in nature. In extreme conditions such as droughts, famines or freezing temperatures, animals activate resilience programs like hibernation instead of investing energy in reproduction. Similarly, aging stem cells appear to divert resources away from producing new cells and toward survival programs as they cope with stress.

"Species survive because they reproduce, but in times of deprivation, animals turn on their own resilience programs," Rando said. "There are a lot of examples in nature of allocating resources to survival under times of stress. It's exactly aligned with what we're seeing at the cellular level."

Implications for Anti Aging Therapies

These findings may guide future therapies designed to boost muscle regeneration in older adults. However, Rando cautions that increasing stem cell performance may come at a price. "There's no free lunch. We can improve the function of aged cells for a period of time, for certain tissues, but every time we do this, there's going to be a potential cost and a potential downside."

The team plans to continue studying how this balance between survival and regeneration is controlled at the molecular level.

"This gene is almost like our doorway that we've opened into understanding what controls these trade-offs that are so critical, not only for evolution of species but also for the aging of tissues within an individual," Rando said.

The study was funded by the National Institutes of Health, the NOMIS Foundation, the Milky Way Research Foundation, the Hevolution Foundation and the National Research Foundation of Korea.
articles.reasoning: This article reports a significant scientific discovery about muscle aging mechanisms in mice, revealing a protein that slows repair but enhances stem cell survival. The findings offer a new understanding of aging and have broad implications for developing therapies to improve muscle regeneration in older adults, representing a meaningful advance in health research with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists uncover how a protein slows muscle repair but helps cells survive in aging mice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As muscles get older, they heal more slowly after injury. This is a problem many older people face, and scientists want to understand why.

**What happened** – Researchers at UCLA studied muscle stem cells in young and old mice. They found that an aging-related protein called NDRG1 builds up in older muscle stem cells. This protein slows down the cells’ ability to start repairing muscle after injury but helps the cells survive longer in the tough environment of aging muscle. When scientists blocked NDRG1 in older mice, their muscle repair became much faster. However, the stem cells didn’t survive as well over time.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that aging is not just about things getting worse. Instead, some changes might help cells survive longer even if they work more slowly. It means the slow repair in older muscles might be a trade-off to keep stem cells alive. This new understanding can change how we think about aging and muscle health.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to study how this balance between survival and repair is controlled at the molecular level. Their goal is to find ways to improve muscle healing in older adults without harming the long-term survival of stem cells.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A protein that slows muscle repair in aging mice also helps stem cells survive longer, revealing a surprising trade-off in how muscles age.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent comment une protéine ralentit la réparation musculaire mais aide les cellules à survivre chez les souris âgées
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Avec l’âge, les muscles guérissent plus lentement après une blessure. C’est un problème que rencontrent de nombreuses personnes âgées, et les scientifiques cherchent à comprendre pourquoi.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’UCLA ont étudié les cellules souches musculaires chez des souris jeunes et âgées. Ils ont découvert qu’une protéine appelée NDRG1 s’accumule dans les cellules des muscles âgés. Cette protéine ralentit la capacité des cellules à commencer la réparation après une blessure, mais aide les cellules à survivre plus longtemps dans l’environnement difficile des muscles vieillissants. Quand les scientifiques ont bloqué NDRG1 chez les souris âgées, la réparation musculaire est devenue beaucoup plus rapide. Cependant, les cellules souches ont moins bien survécu avec le temps.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que le vieillissement n’est pas seulement une détérioration. Certaines modifications aident les cellules à survivre plus longtemps même si elles fonctionnent plus lentement. Cela signifie que la réparation lente des muscles âgés pourrait être un compromis pour garder les cellules souches en vie. Cette nouvelle compréhension peut changer notre façon de penser le vieillissement et la santé musculaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’étudier comment cet équilibre entre survie et réparation est contrôlé au niveau moléculaire. Leur objectif est de trouver des moyens d’améliorer la guérison musculaire chez les personnes âgées sans nuire à la survie à long terme des cellules souches.

**Phrase clé** – Une protéine qui ralentit la réparation musculaire chez les souris âgées aide aussi les cellules souches à survivre plus longtemps, révélant un compromis surprenant dans le vieillissement des muscles.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo una proteína ralentiza la reparación muscular pero ayuda a las células a sobrevivir en ratones envejecidos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que los músculos envejecen, sanan más lentamente después de una lesión. Esto es un problema común en personas mayores, y los científicos quieren entender por qué.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de UCLA estudiaron células madre musculares en ratones jóvenes y viejos. Encontraron que una proteína llamada NDRG1 se acumula en las células musculares de ratones mayores. Esta proteína ralentiza la capacidad de las células para comenzar a reparar el músculo después de una lesión, pero también ayuda a que las células sobrevivan más tiempo en el entorno difícil del músculo envejecido. Cuando los científicos bloquearon NDRG1 en ratones viejos, la reparación muscular fue mucho más rápida. Sin embargo, las células madre sobrevivieron menos con el tiempo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que el envejecimiento no es solo un deterioro. Algunos cambios pueden ayudar a las células a sobrevivir más tiempo aunque funcionen más lento. Esto significa que la reparación lenta en músculos viejos podría ser un intercambio para mantener vivas las células madre. Este nuevo entendimiento puede cambiar cómo pensamos sobre el envejecimiento y la salud muscular.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean estudiar cómo se controla este equilibrio entre supervivencia y reparación a nivel molecular. Su meta es encontrar formas de mejorar la curación muscular en adultos mayores sin dañar la supervivencia a largo plazo de las células madre.

**Frase clave** – Una proteína que ralentiza la reparación muscular en ratones envejecidos también ayuda a las células madre a sobrevivir más tiempo, revelando un intercambio sorprendente en cómo envejecen los músculos.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of a resilient, aged mouse muscle cell depicted as a marathon runner pacing steadily on a softly glowing track, with a subtle, translucent brake symbol gently holding back a burst of vibrant youthful energy represented by a sprinter silhouette fading in the background, all set against a calm, abstract cellular landscape of softly blended natural colors.

articles.title: Landmark vitiligo cream targets immune cells that disrupt pigmentation
articles.summary: A cream that directly disrupts the underlying causes of the skin patches seen in the condition vitiligo will be made available on the NHS
articles.content:
A cream that directly disrupts the underlying causes of the skin patches seen in the condition vitiligo will be made available on the NHS

Vitiligo involves paler, less-pigmented skin patches Getty Images

A first-of-its-kind cream that targets the underlying cause of vitiligo will be made available on the National Health Service in England. In clinical trials, the cream significantly increased pigmentation in the white skin patches caused by the condition, but the treatment has previously been described as controversial, as vitiligo isn’t painful or dangerous.

“Usually, people [with vitiligo] are asymptomatic in terms of physical symptoms, but it can cause a lot of emotional hardship,” says David Rosmarin at Indiana University, who led two trials of the new ruxolitinib cream treatment.

The cream, which is already available in the US, is sold under the name Opzelura. It treats non-segmental vitiligo, which occurs when symmetrical white patches appear on both sides of the body. This is thought to be caused by the immune system attacking melanocytes, the cells that make the pigment melanin, which gives skin colour.

Advertisement

The treatment is the first drug to be robustly tested that acts directly on the pathway that causes vitiligo, says Emma Rush at Vitiligo Support UK. “That’s why this [recommendation] is such a landmark,” she says.

Ruxolitinib works by inhibiting two enzymes that cause immune cells to destroy melanocytes. Existing treatments, like steroid creams, can restore some pigment, but suppress immune function more broadly.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

Two trials, published in 2022, found that the drug increased pigmentation and reduced the noticeability of vitiligo patches compared with a placebo cream. This occurred regardless of people’s skin tones (vitiligo is more noticeable on darker skin), and was maintained for at least a year in more than a third of those who came off the drug after the trial.

The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) weighed up these results in the past, but judged ruxolitinib to not be cost effective for use on the NHS. Now, it says the cream should be made available to people aged 12 and older with non-segmental vitiligo when other topical treatments haven’t worked or are unsuitable.

Vitiligo, which affects about 1 per cent of the global population, varies in severity. Some people have just a few small patches, while for others they can be large, red, inflamed or discoloured.

“Patients and clinicians sometimes think we shouldn’t treat vitiligo, [because] it doesn’t kill you [and] it’s not painful,” says Viktoria Eleftheriadou at the British Association of Dermatologists. But vitiligo can have serious complications, such as a higher risk of depression and anxiety.

Natalie Ambersley – a vitiligo ambassador for the charity Changing Faces, which supports people with physical differences – says that people shouldn’t feel judged for treating the condition, but adds that after spending years using existing treatments, she won’t be seeking out ruxolitinib. “I’ve learned to accept my skin,” she says. “We’re [all] unique and we can embrace what we look like.”

“It’s great that there are people who love the skin they’re in, but that’s not for everyone,” says Rush.

An oral version of ruxolitinib, which is used to treat some cancers and rheumatoid arthritis, has been linked to severe side effects, including lymphoma, heart problems and serious infections. But these have not been reported with the topical version. In the two vitiligo trials, ruxolitinib caused only mild side effects, including acne and itchiness. “There is super minimal systemic absorption,” says Eleftheriadou.

Ruxolitinib is also thought to be safer than steroid creams, which can cause skin thinning with long-term use. People with severe vitiligo may also be offered ultraviolet therapy, but this isn’t widely accessible.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a new, clinically tested cream that targets the root cause of vitiligo, a condition affecting 1% of the global population, and will be made available on the NHS. This treatment offers a significant improvement over existing therapies by specifically inhibiting immune cells that destroy pigment cells, providing a meaningful benefit to many people suffering from the emotional and psychological impacts of vitiligo. The approval by NICE for NHS use marks a substantial positive healthcare advancement with broad public impact.
articles.personality_title: New cream for vitiligo approved for NHS use in England
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Vitiligo is a skin condition that causes white patches to form because the immune system attacks the pigment cells in the skin. It affects about 1% of people worldwide and can cause emotional distress even though it is not painful or dangerous.

**What happened** – A new cream called ruxolitinib, sold as Opzelura, has been approved for use on the National Health Service (NHS) in England. This cream was tested in clinical trials and shown to help restore skin color by stopping the immune system from attacking pigment cells. It will be available for people aged 12 and older with a common form of vitiligo when other treatments have not worked.

**Impact** – This cream is the first treatment to directly target the immune cells that cause vitiligo patches, offering a better option than older treatments like steroid creams. It improved skin color in trial participants of all skin tones and had only mild side effects, such as itchiness and acne. Its approval by the NHS means more people can access a treatment that may reduce the emotional challenges linked to vitiligo.

**What’s next step** – People with vitiligo in England can now talk to their doctors about this new cream if other treatments have failed. Researchers will continue to monitor its long-term effects and safety. The cream’s availability might also encourage similar treatments to be developed and offered in other countries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – England’s NHS will offer a new cream that safely helps restore skin color by targeting the root cause of vitiligo.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle crème pour le vitiligo approuvée pour le NHS en Angleterre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le vitiligo est une maladie de la peau qui provoque des taches blanches parce que le système immunitaire attaque les cellules pigmentaires. Elle touche environ 1 % de la population mondiale et peut causer une souffrance émotionnelle même si elle n’est ni douloureuse ni dangereuse.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle crème appelée ruxolitinib, vendue sous le nom d’Opzelura, a été approuvée pour une utilisation par le National Health Service (NHS) en Angleterre. Cette crème a été testée lors d’essais cliniques et a montré qu’elle aide à restaurer la couleur de la peau en empêchant le système immunitaire d’attaquer les cellules pigmentaires. Elle sera disponible pour les personnes âgées de 12 ans et plus atteintes d’une forme courante de vitiligo lorsque d’autres traitements auront échoué.

**Impact** – Cette crème est le premier traitement ciblant directement les cellules immunitaires responsables des taches de vitiligo, offrant une meilleure option que les anciens traitements comme les crèmes stéroïdes. Elle a amélioré la pigmentation chez les participants de tous types de peau avec seulement des effets secondaires légers, comme des démangeaisons et de l’acné. Son approbation par le NHS signifie que plus de personnes auront accès à un traitement qui peut réduire les difficultés émotionnelles liées au vitiligo.

**Prochaine étape** – Les personnes atteintes de vitiligo en Angleterre peuvent désormais parler à leur médecin de cette nouvelle crème si d’autres traitements ont échoué. Les chercheurs continueront de surveiller ses effets à long terme et sa sécurité. La disponibilité de cette crème pourrait aussi encourager le développement et la disponibilité de traitements similaires dans d’autres pays.

**En une phrase** – Le NHS anglais proposera une nouvelle crème qui aide en toute sécurité à restaurer la couleur de la peau en ciblant la cause du vitiligo.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva crema para el vitiligo aprobada para el NHS en Inglaterra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El vitiligo es una condición de la piel que causa manchas blancas porque el sistema inmunológico ataca las células que producen pigmento. Afecta a alrededor del 1 % de la población mundial y puede causar problemas emocionales aunque no sea doloroso ni peligroso.

**Qué pasó** – Una nueva crema llamada ruxolitinib, que se vende como Opzelura, ha sido aprobada para su uso en el Servicio Nacional de Salud (NHS) en Inglaterra. Esta crema fue probada en ensayos clínicos y demostró que ayuda a restaurar el color de la piel al detener el ataque del sistema inmunológico a las células pigmentarias. Estará disponible para personas de 12 años en adelante con una forma común de vitiligo cuando otros tratamientos no hayan funcionado.

**Impacto** – Esta crema es el primer tratamiento que actúa directamente sobre las células inmunes que causan las manchas de vitiligo, ofreciendo una mejor opción que los tratamientos antiguos como las cremas con esteroides. Mejoró la pigmentación en participantes de todos los tonos de piel y tuvo solo efectos secundarios leves, como picazón y acné. Su aprobación por el NHS significa que más personas podrán acceder a un tratamiento que puede reducir las dificultades emocionales causadas por el vitiligo.

**Próximo paso** – Las personas con vitiligo en Inglaterra pueden ahora hablar con sus médicos sobre esta nueva crema si otros tratamientos no han funcionado. Los investigadores seguirán observando sus efectos a largo plazo y su seguridad. La disponibilidad de esta crema podría también impulsar el desarrollo y la oferta de tratamientos similares en otros países.

**Resumen en una frase** – El NHS de Inglaterra ofrecerá una nueva crema que ayuda a restaurar el color de la piel de forma segura al atacar la causa del vitiligo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two hands gently applying a soothing cream to a stylized patch of skin with fading white spots, surrounded by subtle glowing cells symbolizing immune cells calming down, rendered in soft natural tones of beige, cream, and gentle blues.
